turns.content: I'm trying to set up a fuzz target for our new parser crate using the cargo-fuzz binary, but I keep thinking it's just like running my unit tests in parallel. Is there something fundamentally different about how we invoke these fuzz targets compared to the standard test harness?

That's a very common mental model, and you're right that they look similar on the surface because both use the Test trait. However, the core difference is in how we handle stateful execution and timing constraints. A standard test harness runs each function to completion immediately after setup, whereas libFuzzer integration requires us to implement an FuzzTarget trait where we can choose when to stop or continue based on a counter. The fuzz target needs to be able to detect panics early so the fuzzer doesn't waste cycles on a single bad input that crashes your program repeatedly.

I see, I think I understand you're saying my test harness can't catch those early panics like libFuzzer does. But isn't panic handling just about the Result or Option type? Why do we need this special trait integration instead of just wrapping our function?

You've hit on a key distinction: your test harness expects every test to return a Result, but libFuzzer needs a custom loop that runs until it finds a panic or reaches the corpus limit. The fuzz_target! macro creates a closure that wraps your function and captures its arguments from the global state. This allows us to pass in inputs directly without needing to parse command-line flags like --test-threads=16, which is exactly what we want when debugging production crashes under time pressure.
Wait, I think I'm misunderstanding why the macro is necessary. If cargo-fuzz already handles the corpus management, why can't I just write a regular function and let cargo run it?

That's exactly the common mistake beginners make: thinking the fuzzing infrastructure does all the work for us. The reality is that you must explicitly define how your code interacts with the fuzzer via the FuzzTarget trait, otherwise cargo-fuzz can't inject inputs or read outputs. For example, if you want to print debug info, you need to use println! inside the closure, not a separate test function. This ensures your tooling integrates seamlessly with libFuzzer's corpus management and panic detection without needing extra configuration.

Okay, so I think I get it now—the real issue is that I was trying to write a normal test function instead of using the FuzzTarget trait properly. But what if my parser just panics randomly? How does libFuzzer know which inputs are valuable?

The corpus management is automatic once you define your fuzz_target! macro correctly, but you still need to guide the fuzzer by providing initial seed cases. The key insight here is that libFuzzer will automatically detect panics and record those inputs in a file called .fuzz_corpus, so next run it knows which bad inputs to reuse. This means you don't have to manually curate failing tests; just ensure your parser's panic behavior is deterministic enough for the fuzzer to learn from.
